ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a clearly new combination—"amortized SVGD"—training a neural sampler by backpropagating Stein variational gradient updates to solve Problem 1 (a "wild" VI setting where q_eta(x) is intractable) and applies this to an adversarial MLE (SteinGAN) for energy models. This is distinct from prior SVGD (Liu & Wang 2016) and from existing amortized VI/GAN work: the authors explicitly compare to Ranganath et al. (2016) (a Stein-discrepancy based exception) and Kim & Bengio (2016) (which uses a heuristic diversity regularizer), and argue SVGD provides a principled repulsive term (Sections 3–4). The idea is a meaningful, non-trivial extension of SVGD and GAN/Amortized VI ideas rather than a trivial combination, so it represents notable novelty though it builds directly on recent methods rather than introducing a wholly orthogonal paradigm. | B(4): The paper proposes a novel combination—“amortized SVGD”—that trains black-box neural samplers by backpropagating the Stein variational gradient (Eq.10) to match outputs to SVGD-updated particles (Section 3), enabling inference without tractable q_eta(x) (the “wild variational inference” idea). It further applies this to amortized MLE as SteinGAN (Section 4), framing an adversarial game between a neural sampler and an energy model. The authors clearly distinguish their work from prior art (e.g., SVGD itself, Ranganath et al. 2016, and Kim & Bengio 2016) and introduce a non-trivial, practically useful extension, but the core components (SVGD, amortized inference, GAN-style adversarial training) build on existing methods—hence notable novelty rather than a radical departure.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a clearly new combination—"amortized SVGD"—training a neural sampler by backpropagating Stein variational gradient updates to solve Problem 1 (a "wild" VI setting where q_eta(x) is intractable) and applies this to an adversarial MLE (SteinGAN) for energy models. This is distinct from prior SVGD (Liu & Wang 2016) and from existing amortized VI/GAN work: the authors explicitly compare to Ranganath et al. (2016) (a Stein-discrepancy based exception) and Kim & Bengio (2016) (which uses a heuristic diversity regularizer), and argue SVGD provides a principled repulsive term (Sections 3–4). The idea is a meaningful, non-trivial extension of SVGD and GAN/Amortized VI ideas rather than a trivial combination, so it represents notable novelty though it builds directly on recent methods rather than introducing a wholly orthogonal paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a novel combination—“amortized SVGD”—that trains black-box neural samplers by backpropagating the Stein variational gradient (Eq.10) to match outputs to SVGD-updated particles (Section 3), enabling inference without tractable q_eta(x) (the “wild variational inference” idea). It further applies this to amortized MLE as SteinGAN (Section 4), framing an adversarial game between a neural sampler and an energy model. The authors clearly distinguish their work from prior art (e.g., SVGD itself, Ranganath et al. 2016, and Kim & Bengio 2016) and introduce a non-trivial, practically useful extension, but the core components (SVGD, amortized inference, GAN-style adversarial training) build on existing methods—hence notable novelty rather than a radical departure.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper is overall well organized and follows a logical flow (Intro → SVGD background → Amortized SVGD → Amortized MLE/SteinGAN → Experiments → Conclusion), and the main contributions (amortizing SVGD into a neural sampler and SteinGAN) are stated early in the introduction. Notation is largely consistent and key equations/algorithms (e.g. Eq. (7), updates (9)/(10), and Algorithms 1–2) are clearly presented, which makes the technical narrative readable. However, the provided text contains many duplicated/garbled passages and formatting glitches (repeated paragraphs near the end, stray LaTeX/comments, and references to figures not present), and there are occasional long sentences and minor grammatical issues that reduce clarity in places. Fixing the duplicated content, cleaning up formatting, and ensuring figures/tables are present would make the presentation much stronger. | B(3): The paper is broadly well-organized (intro → method → algorithms → experiments → conclusion) and the main idea (amortizing SVGD and SteinGAN) and Problem 1 are introduced early and repeatedly; Algorithms 1–2 and the SVGD derivation (eqs. (3),(7),(10)) are helpful. However the manuscript contains many duplicated and garbled paragraphs and citation fragments (many repeated lines near the end), which disrupt readability and suggest proofreading/formatting errors. Notation is mostly consistent and key symbols (p(x), f(η;ξ), q0, ∆xi) are defined, but a few small typos/wording issues (e.g., “assess to draws”, occasional grammar) and absent figures/tables in the provided text reduce clarity. Overall the presentation conveys the main ideas but needs careful cleanup and fixing of repeated text and minor language issues.

Notation is largely consistent and key equations/algorithms (e.g. Eq. (7), updates (9)/(10), and Algorithms 1–2) are clearly presented, which makes the technical narrative readable. However, the provided text contains many duplicated/garbled passages and formatting glitches (repeated paragraphs near the end, stray LaTeX/comments, and references to figures not present), and there are occasional long sentences and minor grammatical issues that reduce clarity in places. Fixing the duplicated content, cleaning up formatting, and ensuring figures/tables are present would make the presentation much stronger.

(3),(7),(10)) are helpful. However the manuscript contains many duplicated and garbled paragraphs and citation fragments (many repeated lines near the end), which disrupt readability and suggest proofreading/formatting errors. Notation is mostly consistent and key symbols (p(x), f(η;ξ), q0, ∆xi) are defined, but a few small typos/wording issues (e.g., “assess to draws”, occasional grammar) and absent figures/tables in the provided text reduce clarity. Overall the presentation conveys the main ideas but needs careful cleanup and fixing of repeated text and minor language issues.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The core mathematical pieces (SVGD background (3)-(7), the reparameterization comparison (11), and the identity leading to (12)) are standard and appear correctly applied. The amortized SVGD updates (8)→(9)→(10) are plausible via Taylor linearization and a one-step gradient update, but rely on small-step assumptions and omit discussion of scalar step choices and stability (so the derivation is incomplete for rigorous guarantees). The paper lacks theoretical guarantees that the amortized procedure actually minimizes KL (no convergence or bias analysis for the empirical/kernel-smoothed Δx which itself depends on η), and the experimental methodology uses several heuristic stabilization tricks (Sec.5) with limited ablations, baselines, or statistical evaluation. Overall the technical approach is sound in derivation and implementation plausibility but has missing theoretical analysis and some experimental rigor, so I rate it a 3. | B(3): The core mathematical components (SVGD updates (3)–(7) and the closed-form Stein gradient) are standard and correctly used, and the amortized update derivations (8)–(10) are reasonable approximations (backpropagating the Stein update). However the paper omits important technical caveats: it does not state representational assumptions on f(η;ξ) needed for the amortized sampler to match p, gives no convergence guarantees for the incremental updates, and (10) is presented without discussion of step-scaling or stability. The experimental methodology (Section 5 / Algorithm 2) uses useful baselines (DCGAN) but lacks details and statistical rigor (no error bars, ablations, or clear hyperparameter controls) and relies on several ad-hoc stabilization heuristics (eq.16 and scheduling) that are not theoretically justified.

reviewer_a.justification: The core mathematical pieces (SVGD background (3)-(7), the reparameterization comparison (11), and the identity leading to (12)) are standard and appear correctly applied. The amortized SVGD updates (8)→(9)→(10) are plausible via Taylor linearization and a one-step gradient update, but rely on small-step assumptions and omit discussion of scalar step choices and stability (so the derivation is incomplete for rigorous guarantees). The paper lacks theoretical guarantees that the amortized procedure actually minimizes KL (no convergence or bias analysis for the empirical/kernel-smoothed Δx which itself depends on η), and the experimental methodology uses several heuristic stabilization tricks (Sec.5) with limited ablations, baselines, or statistical evaluation. Overall the technical approach is sound in derivation and implementation plausibility but has missing theoretical analysis and some experimental rigor, so I rate it a 3.

reviewer_b.justification: The core mathematical components (SVGD updates (3)–(7) and the closed-form Stein gradient) are standard and correctly used, and the amortized update derivations (8)–(10) are reasonable approximations (backpropagating the Stein update). However the paper omits important technical caveats: it does not state representational assumptions on f(η;ξ) needed for the amortized sampler to match p, gives no convergence guarantees for the incremental updates, and (10) is presented without discussion of step-scaling or stability. The experimental methodology (Section 5 / Algorithm 2) uses useful baselines (DCGAN) but lacks details and statistical rigor (no error bars, ablations, or clear hyperparameter controls) and relies on several ad-hoc stabilization heuristics (eq.16 and scheduling) that are not theoretically justified.
